frameworks.name: Cialdini's 7 Principles of Persuasion
frameworks.description: Research-backed framework for ethical influence based on human psychology. 95% of people are imitators, only 5% initiators.
frameworks.when_to_use: All marketing campaigns, sales pages, email sequences, and content creation
steps: Reciprocity: Give value first (free guides, samples, insights)
Commitment & Consistency: Get small commitments that lead to larger ones
Social Proof: Show others using/loving your product
Authority: Establish credibility before making requests
Liking: Build similarity, familiarity, and cooperation
Scarcity: Highlight limited availability or time
Unity: Create 'us vs them' mentality with shared identity
frameworks.example: Before asking for email signup, offer valuable free resource (reciprocity), show '10,000+ marketers trust us' (social proof), display founder credentials (authority)

frameworks.name: Consumer Behavior Decision Framework
frameworks.description: 4-factor model explaining purchase decisions: motivation, perception, learning, belief system
frameworks.when_to_use: Campaign planning, audience research, message crafting
steps: Identify core motivation driving target audience
Understand how they perceive your category/brand
Align with their existing belief system
frameworks.example: Fitness app targeting busy professionals: Motivation (efficiency), Perception (time-saving), Learning (past failed gym attempts), Beliefs (health = success)

frameworks.name: Psychological Triggers Hierarchy
frameworks.description: Prioritized list of psychological drivers based on effectiveness research
frameworks.when_to_use: Content creation, ad copy, landing pages
steps: Fear of missing out (FOMO) - highest conversion driver
frameworks.example: Email subject: '[ENDING TONIGHT] 847 marketers joined this week' combines scarcity + social proof

key_principles.explanation: People make decisions based on others' behavior. 95% are imitators, not initiators.
key_principles.application: Lead with user numbers, testimonials, reviews. Show real people using your product. Use phrases like '10,000+ professionals choose...' before product features.

key_principles.explanation: Credibility must be established before influence attempts. Medical diplomas increase patient compliance by 34%.
key_principles.application: Display credentials, awards, media mentions prominently. Have others introduce your expertise. Never self-promote authority - use third-party validation.

key_principles.explanation: People align future behavior with past commitments. Small commitments lead to larger ones.
key_principles.application: Start with micro-commitments (email signup, quiz completion) before major asks. Reference past engagement: 'You downloaded our guide because...'

key_principles.explanation: Limited availability increases perceived value and urgency to act.
key_principles.application: Use specific numbers ('Only 47 spots left'), time limits ('Expires in 6 hours'), or exclusive access ('Members-only'). Avoid vague scarcity.

key_principles.explanation: People feel obligated to return favors. Giving first creates psychological debt.
key_principles.application: Provide valuable content, tools, or insights before asking for anything. Frame as gifts, not lead magnets. Unexpected value works best.

key_principles.explanation: People prefer those similar to themselves. Familiarity breeds liking and trust.
key_principles.application: Mirror audience language, values, and experiences. Share founder stories that match customer backgrounds. Use 'people like you' messaging.

tactics.description: Layer multiple proof types: numbers + testimonials + media mentions + user-generated content
tactics.expected_result: 15-30% increase in conversion rates vs single proof type

tactics.description: Have others introduce your credentials before you speak/write. Never self-promote expertise.
tactics.expected_result: 23% higher trust scores and engagement rates

tactics.description: Start with small asks (email), progress to medium (quiz/assessment), then large (purchase)
tactics.expected_result: 40-60% higher conversion to final offer vs direct ask

tactics.description: Use exact numbers and deadlines. 'Only 23 left' vs 'Limited quantity'
tactics.expected_result: 18% higher urgency response and click-through rates

tactics.description: Give unexpected value before any ask. Frame as gifts, not lead magnets.
tactics.expected_result: 25% higher email signup rates and engagement

tactics.description: Use 'we', 'us', 'our community' language. Create in-group vs out-group dynamics.
tactics.expected_result: 12% higher brand loyalty and repeat purchases

templates.name: Social Proof Opener
templates.template: [NUMBER]+ [TARGET AUDIENCE] [ACTION TAKEN] because [BENEFIT]. Here's why you should too:
templates.use_case: Email subject lines, ad headlines, landing page headers

templates.name: Authority Introduction
templates.use_case: Content introductions, speaker bios, expert quotes

templates.name: Commitment Escalation
templates.template: Since you [PAST ACTION], you're clearly someone who [VALUES/BELIEVES]. That's exactly why [NEXT STEP] makes sense for you.
templates.use_case: Email sequences, sales conversations, upsell offers

templates.name: Scarcity Urgency
templates.template: Only [SPECIFIC NUMBER] [ITEM/SPOTS] remaining. [TIME LIMIT] to secure yours before [CONSEQUENCE].
templates.use_case: Limited offers, event registration, product launches

templates.name: Reciprocity Gift
templates.template: I created [VALUABLE RESOURCE] specifically for [TARGET AUDIENCE] facing [PROBLEM]. It's yours, no strings attached.
templates.use_case: Lead magnets, content offers, relationship building

common_mistakes: Using vague social proof ('many customers') instead of specific numbers
Self-promoting authority instead of having others establish credibility
Asking for big commitments without smaller ones first
Creating fake scarcity that customers can verify as false
Positioning free content as lead magnets instead of genuine gifts
Overusing psychological triggers to the point of manipulation
Ignoring the target audience's existing beliefs and values
Applying tactics without understanding the underlying psychology
pro_tips: Stack multiple psychological triggers but lead with the strongest one for your audience
Test psychological triggers in isolation before combining them
Use specific numbers in social proof - '847 marketers' beats 'hundreds of marketers'
Authority works best when established by others, not self-proclaimed
Reciprocity is most powerful when the gift is unexpected and valuable
Scarcity must be genuine - fake scarcity destroys trust permanently
Mirror your audience's language and values for maximum similarity effect
Commitment escalation works best with 3-step progression: micro, medium, macro
Unity language ('we', 'us', 'our community') builds stronger connections than 'you' language
Combine social proof with authority for maximum credibility impact
